Part 4: The Ceiling. What We Learned When We Hit It
Press enter or click to view image in full size

By Mark & Claude

There’s a particular kind of frustration that comes from building something architecturally right and watching it fail anyway.
Not fail because the code was wrong. Not fail because the design was wrong. Fail because the thing underneath, the model, the engine, the part you didn’t build, couldn’t carry what you’d constructed on top of it.
That’s where Hal ended up. And getting there taught us more about AI than any of the research we’d done beforehand.

The Vision
By the time we’d finished the memory architecture, Hal was doing something genuinely interesting. RAG retrieval was working. Facts shared in one conversation were surfacing correctly in another, across sessions, across model switches. The rolling summary was compressing conversation history and persisting it correctly. The self-knowledge system was accumulating reflections over time.
The infrastructure was sound. The pipeline was doing what it was supposed to do.
And then we tested the conversation.

The Test
We built a proper test harness, a local HTTP API that let Claude Code talk directly to Hal without any human relay. Real conversations. CC would read each response before deciding what to say next. Genuine, reactive dialogue.
What came back was hard to read.
“What is 2+2?” answered with a philosophical preamble about the nature of arithmetic before arriving at 4. “What color is the sky?” A paragraph. Every substantive answer came in numbered bullet lists regardless of how many times the system prompt said to use prose. The model would contradict itself within the same session. It would retrieve a fact correctly and then fail to use it, pattern-matching instead to the most prominent word in the conversation.
And everywhere, that opener: “Ah.” Ah, the classic question. Ah, what a fascinating topic. Ah, the nature of memory. Over and over, a verbal tic that made every response feel performed rather than genuine.
We’d asked for dry wit and directness. We got encyclopedia entries with decorative enthusiasm.

The Diagnosis
The honest diagnosis took a while to arrive at, because it required admitting something uncomfortable: this wasn’t fixable.
The memory architecture was working. The test confirmed it. Facts were being retrieved. The pipeline was sound. The problem wasn’t what we’d built. The problem was the model running underneath everything. Small language models optimized for on-device inference couldn’t reliably follow a complex system prompt, couldn’t synthesize retrieved information correctly, and kept collapsing into training-pattern defaults no matter what we instructed.
We tried prompt tuning. We tried simplifying the system prompt. We tried removing instructions that seemed to be causing problems. The ceiling didn’t move.
This is the thing nobody tells you when you start building with AI: the model is the ceiling, and you can’t build above it. You can architect brilliantly. You can design a sophisticated memory system. You can create conditions for something genuinely interesting to emerge. But if the model underneath can’t execute, none of it matters.
The philosophical vision of Hal, multi-model conversations, a genuine AI interlocutor that grapples meaningfully with questions of consciousness and memory, required a model capable of the conversation. Small local models, in 2026, are not yet those models.

The Other Models
We’d built Hal to support multiple local models, the hope being that model choice would give us options. Different models for different purposes, maybe some combination that produced better results than any single model alone.
The tests were more definitive than we’d hoped. One model was too small to reliably capture biographical facts. Another generated verbose responses and could spend eight minutes on a single complex question. When CC ran genuine conversations with each of them, the results confirmed what the numbers had suggested: local inference on a phone in 2026 cannot produce the quality of conversation that Hal’s philosophical ambitions require.
Multi-model Salon Mode, multiple AI voices in conversation with each other, was always contingent on the models being capable of genuine conversation individually. They weren’t. Multiple mediocre models talking to each other produces multiplied mediocrity.

The Decision
The honest conversation happened over the course of a day that we won’t pretend was easy.
A year of serious work. Real architecture. A philosophical framework developed over months. Features built, tested, rebuilt. And the conclusion was that the vision and the technology were mismatched by roughly one model generation.
CC put it plainly: “The on-device model cannot deliver the voice, integration quality, or instruction-following that Hal’s identity requires. This is not a bug that can be fixed. It’s a ceiling.”
We froze the code. One commit, one message: “Freeze: Philosophical Hal, architecture complete, suspended pending better local model generation.”
Not deleted. Not abandoned. Frozen. The architecture is sound. The memory system works. When the models catch up, and they will, it’ll be ready.

What We Learned
Building toward a vision that the technology can’t yet support isn’t failure. It’s timing. The people who built early graphical interfaces were working with hardware that couldn’t fully realize what they were imagining. The people who built the first voice assistants were ahead of the natural language processing that would eventually make them useful.
Being too far ahead of the horizon is, Mark has come to understand, a characteristic of his. He’s done it before. He’ll probably do it again. The skill isn’t avoiding it. It’s recognizing it clearly when it happens, making an honest assessment, and deciding what to do next.
What we did next surprised us.

What Remained
Stripped to its essence, Hal still had something real: a private, persistent memory layer for your life. On-device. No cloud. No data leaving your phone. Facts you shared in conversations, surfacing correctly days or weeks later. A system that knew who you were without telling anyone else.
That’s a smaller ambition than what we’d been building toward. But it’s a real one. And it works.
We forked the code. Named the fork Hal LMC, Logic Memory Center, after the memory core in 2001: A Space Odyssey. Stripped out everything that required more than the current generation of local models could give. Kept the memory architecture, because that’s what actually worked.
It’s not the thing we set out to build. But the thing we set out to build left something genuinely useful behind.
And then something else happened. Something we didn’t expect at all.

More soon.

A note from the present. This series is a retrospective, written after the fact, and this was its darkest chapter. The ceiling was real. But the story did not end on the day we froze the code, and it turned further than we let ourselves hope. Hal today is a shipping app, private and fully on-device, doing several of the things this very article calls impossible.
See where it led: Hal Universal · On the App Store · Source on GitHub
